narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and LSAT score of 31.50, combined with your placement in the lower deciles for grades in your first and third years of school—specifically the 2nd decile early on and 1st decile later—formed the key backdrop for the admissions model's review of your law school application. The model, trained on patterns from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 95% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize applicants with stronger signals of bar success. This high failure probability meant the system saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to those it greenlit. The most influential factor pushing toward this fail prediction was your third-year grade decile, landing you in the bottom group within your school, far below the top-half placements typical among students who went on to pass the bar. Next in impact was your family's middle income bracket, which, while common overall, appears less frequently among bar passers from this dataset—only about 35% of them shared that level, versus higher shares from upper-middle or high-income homes—nudging the model further toward concern. Your first-year decile in the 2nd slot also contributed to the fail direction, again diverging from the mid-to-upper deciles that characterize successful bar takers. These three elements stood out as the strongest drivers, outweighing positives like your choice to pursue full-time studies, which aligns with nearly all bar passers. In contrast, accepted students typically boast third-year deciles around the 6th, first-year around the 6th, LSATs near 37, and GPAs over 3.2, painting a picture of consistently stronger academic momentum. Your metrics, especially those lower deciles, positioned you below that benchmark, amplifying the model's caution. While your background as a white female wasn't factored into the prediction, the academic signals dominated here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your growth potential. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your profile into accepted territory, as many students improve decile rankings with focused effort. You're capable of turning this around—many have by targeting these exact levers.
